repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
This PR transitions the embedded terminal's underlying library from libvterm to libghostty-vt, as discussed in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/33155.

The PR is structured to maintain a fully working terminal at every commit. To do this, I tried to identify a number of independent subsystems that could be gradually ported to use the new ghostty-provided APIs while maintaining full functionality. These turned out to be focus reporting, bracketed paste, mouse and keyboard input encoding, the responses to various terminal queries, the rendering of terminal text and hl attributes in the visible area, the handling of lines in scrollback (and a few other minor ones).

While the final diff count is pretty large, this approach should have the added benefit of making each commit relatively easy to review. The tradeoff is that in some cases I had to write temporary workarounds to avoid duplicate or overlapping functionality, which were removed in later commits. While that adds churn, I still considered it to be preferable to having fewer, larger commits.

In terms of functionality, this shouldn't result in any substantial user-visible change, although I did take the opportunity to fix a number of bugs along the way (see list of closed issues below). The one important exception to this is that we now have proper reflowing of lines in the scrollback region, finally closing [this](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/4997) 10 year old issue.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a86cfe44-f732-4995-9e96-16f1da93d60c

Note that the Cmake dependency is currently pinned to my fork of ghostty because it contains fixes for a number of bugs that were discovered by our test suite. I opened these 5 PRs to upstream the fixes, so we have to wait for all of them to be merged:

- [x] https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/12581
- [x] https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/12596
- [x] https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/12598
- [ ] https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/12631
- [x] https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/pull/12653

AI disclosure: I used GPT-5.5 extensively in the initial research phase to understand how Neovim's embedded terminal worked, how it used the libvterm APIs, how those differed from libghostty's, etc.

I also let it fix failing tests during the transition. Most of these failures were caused by tests asserting details that were specific to libvterm's behaviour, such as resize timing, scrollback snapshots, cursor positions after resize reports, reflowed line layout, highlight defaults, etc. When doing this, I prompted it to only update a test when the old expectation was outdated under the new libghostty-backed implementation, and to report back instead of changing the test if the failure looked like an actual implementation bug.

While I reviewed its changes, I'd still appreciate it if someone more familiar with the history of the terminal test suite could double check them.

The actual code changes in `terminal.c` were written by me.

Closes (at least):

- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38408 (this PR is based on it, so s/o to @dundargoc for getting the ball rolling);
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/37800
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/37104
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/33155
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/28121
- https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/4997

comment_data.body:
Maybe make this a macro instead?

```c
#define GHOSTTY_ASSERT(res) assert((res) == GHOSTTY_SUCCESS)
```
This shouldn't be related to vterm or libghostty. Can you separate this change out into a separate PR (and describe the problem and solution)?
Nice

@@ -197,7 +197,6 @@ The externally maintained libraries used by Neovim are:
   - libmpack: MIT license
   - libtermkey: MIT license
   - libuv. Copyright Joyent, Inc. and other Node contributors. Node.js license.
-  - libvterm: MIT license

comment_data.body:
Shouldn't this use render_state rather than grid_ref? Same goes for usage of ghostty_grid_ref_graphemes and similar functions. From a comment in the C header for grid_ref:
```C
 * This API is not meant to be used as the core of render loop. It isn't 
 * built to sustain the framerates needed for rendering large screens. 
 * Use the render state API for that. 
```
Iterating whole rows at once should also be more efficient than grabbing one cell per function call as you do in some places. In my implementation, I only ever replaced whole lines in the grid.
done in e7bf16734732
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -197,7 +197,6 @@ The externally maintained libraries used by Neovim are:
   - libmpack: MIT license
   - libtermkey: MIT license
   - libuv. Copyright Joyent, Inc. and other Node contributors. Node.js license.
-  - libvterm: MIT license

comment_data.body: fixed in a03589021752
I suspect we should also use render_state here. It still bogs down more than it should.

comment_regular_data.body: I see a handful of functional tests are failing, but I can't replicate any of them locally. They all pass on my mac, I guess they're not deterministic?

> I see a handful of functional tests are failing, but I can't replicate any of them locally. They all pass on my mac, I guess they're not deterministic?

? It's not normal for multiple CI jobs to fail, even on the most "flaky" days.

> finally closing [this](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/4997) 10 year old issue

Tried this PR locally. The softwrap behavior is definitely much better!

But there is a performance issue somewhere.

```
nvim
:vertical terminal

# in :terminal
cat test/functional/fixtures/bigfile.txt

:9 wincmd |

# in :terminal, do this 10+ times:
cat test/functional/fixtures/bigfile.txt

:only
```

with the above steps, each `cat test/functional/fixtures/bigfile.txt` gets slower and slower. and the `:only` also is very slow. I guess some internal state in `ghostty` is retained somewhere, even though 'scrollback' was exceeded (thus the lines could be pruned at some point).

> It's not normal for multiple CI jobs to fail, even on the most "flaky" days

They seem to be consistent within the CI environment, but I still can't get them to fail on my machine. Do they fail on yours?

> But there is a performance issue somewhere.

Should be much better after 28bcc8f4d9fa. It's still slower than it should be because we're overallocating scrollback, see [this](https://github.com/noib3/neovim/blob/28bcc8f4d9fa4ace3bb81a63478b9306adaee995/src/nvim/terminal.c#L858-L863).

> we're overallocating scrollback, see [this](https://github.com/noib3/neovim/blob/28bcc8f4d9fa4ace3bb81a63478b9306adaee995/src/nvim/terminal.c#L858-L863).

is that something in ghostty's issue tracker, if so can we link the issue, or if not do we need to tell them?

I don't think there's an issue about it, so we should probably tell them.

Another problem with that is that ghostty's `max_scrollback` can only be set once at initialization, whereas our `scrollback` value is dynamic (which is why I'm currently using `SB_MAX` as the line count, which makes the performance issue even worse).

Just for reference, the [perf issue](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39773#issuecomment-4450586797) doesn't seem improved in the latest revision of this PR. It might not be a blocker, if everything else here looks solid. But we'll want to have some sort of plan.

`clear` (in shell) is a workaround, but the scrollback fills up again quickly when running builds/tests...

> ghostty's `max_scrollback` can only be set once at initialization

well, we can just set it to whatever we consider the "max" for performance purposes.

If >10k scrollback cases per issues, then that's the max we can afford to "reflow". We can support a higher *non-reflow* max scrollbac, which we manage ourselves. But we tell ghostty a smaller max scrollback, so that it doesn't attempt to reflow 100k or 1m lines.

WDYT?

@mitchellh does it sound right that we are seeing perf issues for reflow of 10k lines of scrollback? Is it possible we're doing something wrong that is causing this?

> the [perf issue](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39773#issuecomment-4450586797) doesn't seem improved in the latest revision of this PR

Really? To notice any slowdown I had to `cat test/functional/fixtures/bigfile.txt` ~10 times before 28bcc8f4d9fa and ~25 times after it.

> > the [perf issue](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39773#issuecomment-4450586797) doesn't seem improved in the latest revision of this PR
> 
> Really? To notice any slowdown I had to `cat test/functional/fixtures/bigfile.txt` ~10 times before [28bcc8f](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/commit/28bcc8f4d9fa4ace3bb81a63478b9306adaee995) and ~25 times after it.

maybe. but I've been running this PR as my daily driver (with `scrollback=100000`, same as I usually do), and it's very easy to hit the perf issue.

there is also a weird case where the scrollback gets blanked out (the buffer is filled with empty lines) if I open a new window. don't have a repro yet.

Introducing `libghostty-vt` into neovim as an **external** dependency means we need making zig as a required dependency, _even when_ compiling with clang/g++.

And from what I've discovered, `ghostty`'s strategy is using a **specific** zig version (0.15.2 in this case) and conflict with our zig minimal version (0.16.0). So either we ~downgrade our `build.zig`~ or wait until https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/issues/12228 is resolved.

Also, once we get the versions aligned, ghostty still needs to be added to `build.zig` itself (headers + linking) to make ci happy.

---

BTW, looks like the `libghostty-vt` job in `.github/workflows/build.yml` might not be necessary. The `test.yml` functionaltest jobs already cover it I think.

> And from what I've discovered, ghostty's strategy is using a specific zig version (0.15.2 in this case) and conflict with our zig minimal version (0.16.0). So either we downgrade our build.zig or wait until https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/issues/12228 is resolved.

Yeah, we intended to keep up with the latest Zig version, so we'll be on 0.16 soon. But its pretty impossible at the moment with Zig to support multiple versions at once so for now that's our versioning strategy. I'm open to any suggestions here.

Just tried out the changes. cat'ing the big file is absolutely better in RelWithDebInfo mode, but not as good as it could be. It's basically unusable on my system in Debug mode when there's a lot of output. The Zig one I wrote does get slower if I increase the scrollback to 1000000, fill it up, and reflow, but still not quite as slow, it looks like. I haven't checked out the code too much, so I'm not sure what problems there might be. However, I think clearing your scrollback that exceeds the nvim scrollback will help a fair amount.

> > we're overallocating scrollback, see [this](https://github.com/noib3/neovim/blob/28bcc8f4d9fa4ace3bb81a63478b9306adaee995/src/nvim/terminal.c#L858-L863).
> 
> is that something in ghostty's issue tracker, if so can we link the issue, or if not do we need to tell them?

where is the upstream issue for this? this looks like it will require a breaking change in libghostty so it is time-sensitive...
> I don't think there's an issue about it

> can you create one?

Created [this](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/discussions/12769) discussion.

> @noib3 can't you just delete from ghostty scrollback when it exceeds nvim scrollback?

AFAICT there's currently no public C API to do that. I've created [this other](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/discussions/12770) discussion for it, which could also remove the need to implement the non-reflow scrollback behavior suggested in https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/39773#issuecomment-4461537354.

title: feat(terminal): replace `libvterm` with `libghostty-vt`
